scenes.text:
The caravan moves at the pace of a tired ox, which is to say: barely. Three wagons, eleven souls, one dog that has decided you are its problem now. The dust gets into everything — your hair, your teeth, the spaces between your thoughts.
A girl no older than twelve walks beside your wagon. She has a tin cup at her belt and eyes that have learned not to ask twice. "I sell stories," she says, matter-of-fact. "One cup of water, one story. I know good ones."
Behind her, an old woman is humming a song you almost remember from somewhere — maybe your mother, maybe a dream. The dog leans against your leg.
The sun is a slow knife. You have a long road ahead, and the road has things to say.

choices.text: Buy a story from the girl.
Watch the horizon. Keep your water.

scenes.text:
You tip a swallow of water into her cup. She drinks it like a prayer — slow, eyes closed — and then she sits cross-legged on the wagon boards and tells you about a boy who fell in love with a door.
"He loved it so much he forgot what was on the other side," she says. "That's the trick of doors. They make you forget there's a room."
She looks at you sidelong. "You're going to one, aren't you. A door. I can tell. People going to doors walk funny."
You don't answer. She nods like you did.
"My cousin went," she adds, quieter. "Last spring. Hasn't come back. If you see a boy with a chipped front tooth out there — his name is Pell — tell him Juno is still saving him a seat at supper."
The dust goes on forever. So does her small, stubborn face.

do: journal:A girl named Juno asked me to find her cousin Pell. Chipped tooth. Still saving him a seat.

choices.text: Tell her the truth — most don't come back.

scenes.text:
"I'll look," you say, and you mean it in the way you mean things when you're tired enough to be honest.
Juno doesn't smile. She just nods once, like she's adding your name to a small list she keeps. Then she gets up and goes to sell another story two wagons down.
The dog sighs and puts its chin on your boot. Somewhere ahead, the road bends around a ridge of black rock that looks, in this light, like a sleeping animal.
You wonder if Pell ever sat where you're sitting. If he watched the same ridge and thought the same thing.
The wagon creaks on.

choices.text: Walk up to the lead wagon.

scenes.text:
"Most don't come back, Juno."
She looks at you a long time. Not hurt. Older than that. "I know," she says. "I'm not stupid. I just need somebody to keep looking anyway."
She takes her cup and goes. The dog stays. The dog, apparently, has standards and has chosen you despite your mouth.
You sit with what you said. It was true. It was also unnecessary. There is a difference, you are learning, between the truth and a thing that needed saying.

choices.text: Let it go. Watch the road.

scenes.text:
You catch her at the back of the third wagon. She doesn't look up.
"I'll still look for him," you say. "I shouldn't have said it like that."
She shrugs the shrug of a child who has been disappointed by adults professionally. Then, after a moment: "Okay."
That's it. That's the whole forgiveness. You take it and feel obscurely lighter.
The dog, watching this whole exchange, thumps its tail once in approval.

do: journal:Juno forgave me, more or less. I owe her a boy with a chipped tooth.

scenes.text:
The old woman makes room for you on her bench without looking up. Her humming is a thread you can almost follow.
"My mother used to —" you start.
"Yes," she says. "Lots of mothers did. It's an old song. Older than the kingdom." She pats your knee like she's known you all your life. "It's about a girl who goes looking for her brother and finds something else instead. The song doesn't say what."
She hums another bar. Your eyes prickle without permission.
"You're looking for someone," she says. Not a question.
"Yes."
"Then I'll sing for you tonight," she says, and goes back to humming. The road softens at the edges.

do: journal:An old woman sang my mother's song. I didn't know I still knew the words.

scenes.text:
You keep your water. You watch the horizon.
It does what horizons do — it pretends not to move while moving. By midafternoon the heat warps it into shapes: a tower, a horse, a person waving. Your eyes invent and your eyes uninvent.
A man on the second wagon, a trader with rope-burn scars on his hands, slides down to walk beside you. "First time crossing?"
"Yes."
"The trick," he says, "is to pick one thing on the horizon and decide it's real. Even if it isn't. Gives your eyes somewhere to live." He grins, missing a tooth. "Mine's a woman in a blue dress. She's been waiting for me for nine crossings."
You pick something on the horizon. You don't tell him what.

do: journal:I picked {missing_short} on the horizon. {missing_pron_cap} is the thing I have decided is real.

scenes.text:
The lead wagon is driven by a wiry woman named Tobish who has, you learn in the first minute, opinions about everything and patience for almost none of it.
"You're the one going to the Gate," she says, not asking. "Don't bother lying, child. You all have the same walk." She spits over the side. "I'll get you to the foothills. After that you're on your own and the wastes are honest — they kill quick."
She eyes your pack. "You short on supplies?"
The honest answer would cost you pride. The dishonest answer would cost you food.

Tobish snorts and tosses you a wrapped bundle without looking. "Don't make a thing of it. I won't have it said I let a fool starve on my wagon."
It's dried meat, hard biscuit, a twist of salt. More than you expected. You start to thank her and she waves you off like a fly.
"My brother went to the Gate," she says, eyes on the road. "Years ago now. Came back wrong, then didn't come back at all. So." She shrugs. "So I keep the wagon going. People still need to get places."
She doesn't look at you again. You climb down from the lead wagon with the bundle in your arms, and a hot, complicated feeling in your chest.

do: journal:Tobish lost a brother to the Gate. She still drives the wagon. I think that's a kind of courage I don't have a name for.

scenes.text:
Night drops fast in the wastes. One moment everything is amber and the next the sky is purple and the stars are showing off.
They light a fire low and shielded — a habit, the trader explains, from years when bandits read smoke like a menu. Someone passes a flask. Someone tells a story about a goat. The humming woman sings, and her voice is small and steady and exactly the shape of the ache under your ribs.
Across the fire, a boy of maybe fifteen is staring at you. Not unfriendly. Just staring. He has the look of someone working up to something.
The stars get sharper. The fire crackles. The boy opens his mouth, closes it, opens it again.

He comes around the fire too fast and sits too close and immediately looks like he regrets both. "You're going to the Gate."
"Everyone keeps saying that."
"Because it's true." He picks at a hole in his sleeve. "My sister works in the kitchens. At the palace. She — she said the {missing_short_cap} was kind. To everyone. To the scullery girls and the boys who sweep. She said {missing_pron} learned all their names."
He looks up at you, and his eyes are wet, and very young.
"If you find {missing_obj}. Tell {missing_obj} someone in the wastes still remembers {missing_poss} name. Tell {missing_obj} my sister's name was Pell — no, sorry — my sister's name is Mira. Tell {missing_obj} Mira said thank you."
The fire pops. The humming woman sings on.

do: journal:Mira, scullery girl. Said thank you. I am to deliver this to {missing_short}.

scenes.text:
Day two is wind.
The wind starts gentle, the way bad things often do. By midmorning it's a wall. The wagons stop. The dog whines. The trader curses with impressive vocabulary, and Tobish is already shouting orders — tarps down, faces wrapped, hold the horses, hold each other, the sand is coming.
It comes.
It is brown and red and the sound of a million tiny teeth. You can't see the wagon two paces away. You can't see your own hand. Someone screams and is cut short — not hurt, just swallowed by sound.
A hand grabs yours. Small. Cold. Juno.
"Don't let go," she shouts into the gale. "Promise."

do: journal:I held Juno through the storm. She didn't cry. I almost did.

scenes.text:
The storm lasts an hour. It lasts a year. You can't tell.
When it stops, the world is reshaped. New dunes. Old tracks erased. The horizon is a stranger.
One wagon is on its side. The horses are alive, mostly. The humming woman is alive, mostly. The trader has a gash on his forehead that he is treating with what looks like wine and what is definitely cursing.
Juno is still holding your hand. She lets go like it embarrasses her.
"You kept your promise," she says. Like she'd been keeping score.
Tobish takes one look at the dunes and her mouth goes flat. "We've lost the road," she says. "We'll find it. Probably."

choices.text: Tend the trader's wound with poultice.
do: journal:I patched the trader. He insists on paying me back in stories I won't live long enough to hear.

scenes.text:
The second night is quieter, the way nights are after disasters — everyone too tired to perform.
You sit a little apart from the fire because your skin feels too small. The stars are absurd out here, a spilled bowl of them, more sky than dark.
Footsteps. Tobish, with two cups. She hands you one without speaking. It's the cheap, hot kind of liquor that tastes like a small fire and a kind grandmother.
"You did all right today," she says, and that's the whole of her speech, and she sits beside you and drinks, and the silence is companionable. After a while she says, "What's {missing_poss} name?" and you realize she means the one you're going for, and you say it, and she nods like she's filing it away with her brother's.

choices.text: Ask her if she dreams of him.

scenes.text:
She is quiet a long time.
"Every night," she says finally. "He's always a boy in the dreams. Never the man he became. I think the dreams know something I don't."
She drinks. The stars don't move and also do.
"If you find your {missing_short}, child — and I think you might, you have the look — don't ask {missing_obj} what happened in there. Not at first. Just feed {missing_obj}. Let {missing_obj} sleep. Sit beside {missing_obj}. The questions will keep."
She stands, knees popping. "And don't be a hero. Heroes die. Be stubborn. Stubborn comes home."

do: journal:Tobish said: don't be a hero, be stubborn. I'm going to try.

scenes.text:
Day three. You can smell the foothills before you see them — cold stone, thin pine, something almost like rain.
Midmorning, the trader comes to you, and he won't meet your eyes.
"Listen," he says, soft. "I took something from your pack. Last night. Just — a coin. Just one. My boy back in the city, he's — listen, I'm telling you because I put it back. Couldn't sleep. I'm an idiot. I'm sorry."
He holds out his hand. A single copper, warm from his pocket.
It is a betrayal small enough to be forgivable. It is also a betrayal.

do: journal:He stole a coin and gave it back. I am keeping that, and him.

choices.text: Tell him to keep it. Send it to his boy.
Be cold. Take it. Walk away.

scenes.text:
By dusk on the third day the wagons stop at the lip of the foothills. Tobish swings down and starts unhitching like she's done it a thousand times because she has.
"This is me," she says. "You go up. We go around. May we meet again on a less ugly road."
Juno presses something into your hand — a flat river stone, smooth, painted with a small, careful J. "For luck," she mutters, and walks off before you can answer.
The humming woman waves. The trader salutes. The dog watches you go, and you have to not look back or you will not leave.
Ahead: the road bends up into stone. Somewhere up there, a rider waits, or doesn't. A hill to search. A tower. A Gate.

choices.text: Take the road up to the rider.
do: journal:I have Juno's stone in my pocket. It weighs more than a stone should.

choices.text: Detour to the wayhouse for stew.
